q: Lingering thermal pain and spontaneous pain often indicate:
Brief provoked thermal pain that does not linger suggests:
options: Necrosis with no response ever only possibility

q: No response to vitality tests + draining sinus tract often fits:
options: Pulp necrosis with chronic apical abscess pattern

q: Symptomatic apical periodontitis features:
options: Pain to biting/percussion with apical inflammation

q: Acute apical abscess features:
options: Rapid swelling, pain, possible systemic signs

q: Narrowest canal diameter historically targeted for WL is:
Primary tissue-dissolving irrigant is:
EDTA is used mainly to:
options: Dissolve all pulp tissue alone better than NaOCl

q: Mixing NaOCl and chlorhexidine in the canal is avoided because:
Persistent symptoms after RCT of maxillary molar — think missing:
options: Always only periodontal cause never endo

q: Rotary file separation when tip locks and shaft keeps rotating is classic:
options: Only cyclic fatigue in straight canals always

q: File fracture from repeated flexure in curved canals relates to:
Instrument retrieval when indicated may use:
options: Ultrasonics / instrument removal systems under magnification
Only larger Gates blindly always first and only

q: Modern apical barrier material for apexification often cited is:
options: Amalgam apical plug only classic now
Pure calcium hydroxide only forever without barrier option

q: Apexogenesis aims to:
options: Kill pulp then place MTA barrier only definition
Preserve vital pulp tissue to allow continued root development

q: Apexification is for:
options: Nonvital immature tooth needing apical barrier/closure approach

q: Vertical root fracture clinical clues include:
options: Only white lesion that wipes off
Always panorex only finding without clinical
Isolated deep pocket, J-shaped lesion, often post-endo tooth

q: Internal resorption requires:
External inflammatory resorption often linked to:
Avulsed permanent tooth best immediate action by layperson often:
options: Scrub root dry and wrap in tissue only
Replant if possible or store in suitable medium; seek urgent care

q: Preferred storage media concepts include:
options: Milk / Hank's / saliva as available options better than dry

q: Flexible splint duration after many avulsion protocols is often about:
Lateral luxation means:
options: Displacement of tooth buccally/lingually/mesially/distally in socket

q: Concussion injury means:
options: Injury without displacement or increased mobility; tender

q: Subluxation means:
Intrusion of permanent tooth is:
options: Serious luxation into bone; complex management

q: Working length determination tools include:
Rubber dam in endo is:
Overfill beyond apex risks:
Sealer purpose includes:
options: Replace all gutta-percha always alone without core
Fill irregularities / improve seal with core material

q: Hot tooth anesthesia strategies may include:
options: Supplemental injections (PDL, intraosseous, intrapulpal) when IANB fails

q: Access goals include:
Perforation repair modern material often:
options: Only unfilled composite without isolation always

q: Ledging is often caused by:
options: Forcing stiff instruments short of curvature

q: Smear layer is:
options: Organic + inorganic debris on canal walls after instrumentation

q: Primary teeth avulsion management differs because:
options: Always replant primary same as permanent
Replantation of primary teeth is generally not recommended

q: Radiographic J-shaped lesion adjacent to post-retained crown raises concern for:
Pulp vitality testing limitation includes:
Calcium hydroxide historically used for:
Cracked tooth syndrome often presents as:
options: Pain on release of bite / selective loading patterns

q: EDTA then NaOCl sequence conceptually:
options: Remove smear then further disinfection protocols as taught

q: Cvek pulpotomy concept is:
options: Full RCT always for every chip
Partial pulpotomy for traumatic exposures with healthy pulp

q: Root fracture coronal third often needs:
options: Stabilization considerations / endo of coronal segment if necrotic

q: Alveolar fracture management includes:
options: Reposition and splint segments per trauma protocols
Only endo of all teeth always first without reposition

q: Pulp canal obliteration after trauma means:
options: Calcific metamorphosis; may still need monitoring

q: Sealer extrusion into IAN region is:
options: Serious; may cause neuropathy — prevention critical

q: Crown-down instrumentation advantages include:
options: Early coronal flare, better irrigant exchange, reduced debris extrusion concepts

q: Patency filing concept is:
options: Gently maintain apical patency to avoid blocks (technique-dependent teaching)
Force large files past apex always

q: Eugenol-containing materials and resin bonding:
options: Eugenol can interfere with resin polymerization

q: Indication for endodontic microsurgery may include:
options: Persistent apical disease after adequate orthograde therapy
explanation: Surgery after failed orthograde when indicated.
